draft.introduction: A creation hymn paralleling Genesis 1 in poetic form. It celebrates God’s ongoing sustenance of creation and bears striking resemblances to the Egyptian Hymn to Aten.

translation.text: Bless the Lord, O my soul! O Lord my God, you are very great. You are clothed with splendor and majesty, covering yourself with light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent.
translation.original_text: εὐλόγει ἡ ψυχή μου τὸν κύριον κύριε ὁ θεός μου ἐμεγαλύνθης σφόδρα

translation.text: When you hide your face, they are dismayed; when you take away their breath, they die and return to dust. When you send forth your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the face of the ground.
translation.original_text: ἀποστρέψαντος δέ σου τὸ πρόσωπον ταραχθήσονται

translator_notes.note: “You send forth your Spirit” (ἐξαποστελεῖς τὸ πνεῦμά σου) was read by the Fathers as a reference to the Holy Spirit’s ongoing creative work, connecting creation theology to pneumatology.
